• One Species (Fisher-KPP Family)
    • Fisher-KPP Equation
      • Allen-Cahn Equation
      • Two species
        • Symmetry
          • ∂ui∂t=Di∂2ui∂x2+fi(u)
          ∂tui=Di∇x2ui+fi(u)

          One Species (Fisher-KPP Family)

          Fisher-KPP Equation

          ∂u∂t=D∂2u∂x2+ru(1−au)

          let u′=au, τ=rat, z=arDx,

          ∂u′∂τ=∂2u′∂z2+u′(1−u′)

          Now we focus on

          ∂u∂t=∂2u∂x2+u(1−u)

          Assume a traveling wave solution, where the shape of u(x,t) do not change. let z=x−ct,

          d2udz2+cdudz+u(1−u)=0

          u=0,1 are two equilibrium of f(u)=u(1−u).The solution will connect this two state. When u→0, assume the approximated solution around here is u≈eλz, thus,

          λ2+cλ+1=0

          λ=−c±c2−42. u→0 needs λ<0, which calls c≥2.

          Fisher's method

          let p=dudz,

          dpdu=−c−u(1−u)p

          assume

          pu|u→0=m

          then

          m=dpdu=−c−u(1−u)p≈−c−1mm2+cm+1=0

          has real roots only if c2≥4, thus c≥2.

           

          Then we show the dynamics of p∼u on the phase plane,

          {u˙=pp˙=−cp−u(1−u)

          equilibrium are (p∗,u∗)=(0,0),(0,1).

          J=(−c2u−110)
          J|(0,0)=(−c−110),J|(0,1)=(−c110)

          For (0,0)

          λ2−(−c)λ+1=0

          Δ=c2−4≥0, λ=−c±c2−42>0 , is a stable node

           

          For (0,1)

          λ2−(−c)λ−1=0

          Δ=c2+4≥0, λ=−c±c2+42 , is a saddle.

          There is a trajectory connecting these two equilibrium, from (0,0) to (0,1). Let this curve be p=φ(u), then dudz=φ(u), or dudx=φ(u). Thus,

          x−x0=∫u0u1φ(u)du

          and we will finally get u(z).

          As a result, the travelling wave solution for the PDE is the solution of the ODE with boundary condition

          limz→+∞u(z)=0,limz→−∞u(z)=1

          To explore the shape, we may use small perturbation method. Assume c→∞, and let ξ=z/c,

          1c2d2udξ2+dudξ+u(1−u)=0

          We may assume the solution is in a form of series,

          u(ξ)=u0(ξ)+1c2u1(ξ)+O(1c4)

          then by the power of c,

          du0dξ+u0(1−u0)=0d2u0dξ2+du1dξ+u1(1−2u0)=0

          If we assume u(0)=1/2, then we also have u0=1/2, u1=0. Then we could solve them,

          u0=11+ez/cu1=ez/c(1+ez/c)2ln⁡(4ez/c(1+ez/c)2)

          u≈u0+c−2u1

          This approximation fits well even c=2.

          Allen-Cahn Equation

          ∂u∂t=∂2u∂x2+u(1−u)(u−a)
          d2udz2+cdudz+u(1−u)(u−a)=0
          {u˙=pp˙=−cp−u(1−u)(u−a)
          J=(−c3u2−2(1+a)u+a10)
          J|(0,0)=(−ca10),J|(0,1)=(−c1−a10)J|(0,a)=(−ca(a−1)10)

          At (0,0), tr(J)=−c<0, det(J)=−a<0 , this node is a saddle. Δ=c2+4a>0

          At (1,0), tr(J)=−c<0, det(J)=−(1−a)<0 , this node is a saddle. Δ=c2+4(1−a)>0

          At (a,0), tr(J)=−c<0, det(J)=−a(a−1)=a(1−a)>0 , this node is stable. Δ=c2−4a(1−a), λ=−c±c2−4a(1−a)2, could be stable node or spiral sink, depends on the value of Δ.

          As a result, the travelling wave solution only exist when a certain speed c makes a trajectory connecting (0,0) and (1,0).

           

          Considering the symmetry of (0,0) and (0,1) , we take the condition where a=1/2. In order to have a trajectory connecting two states, c=0.

          Then we solve the equation

          d2udz2=u(1−u)(a−u)d2udz2=u3−(1+a)u2+au12(dudz)2=14u4−1+a3u3+a2u22(dudz)2=u2(1−u)2
          dudz=22u(1−u)1u(1−u)du=22dz(1u+11−u)du=22dzln⁡u−ln⁡(1−u)=z2+Cu1−u=ez2+Cu=11+ez2+Cu0=1/2⇒C=0u=11+ez2
          dpdu=−c−u(1−u)(u−a)p

           

          A general way to considering this kind of equation is considering the potential energy.

          We start from the ODE

          d2udz2+cdudz+u(1−u)(u−a)=0

          We hope to define such a function E(u) as the energy of certain density u, such that

          dE(u)dt≤0

          That is, E(u) always decline to a lower value. Note here t is just a assumed time for the evolution of E, not the time in the former PDE. Here, for example, we could assume z in the equation is t. As a result, we try to find a dEdz.

          Note the following relation holds:

          d2udz2⋅dudz=12⋅d(dudz)2dz

           

          Thus, we multiply the derivative to the ODE:

          (d2udz2+cdudz+u(1−u)(u−a))⋅dudz=012ddz(dudz)2+c(dudz)2+u(1−u)(u−a)⋅dudz=0

          let

          f(u)=u(1−u)(u−a)=dWdu
          W(u)=∫u(1−u)(u−a)u=−14u4+1+a3u3−a2u2

          where W(0)=0. W(1)=112−a6

          then

          12ddz(dudz)2+c(dudz)2+u(1−u)(u−a)dudz=012ddz(dudz)2+c(dudz)2+dWdu⋅dudz=012ddz(dudz)2+c(dudz)2+dWdz=0
          ddz(12(dudz)2+W)=−c(dudz)2≤0

           

          We define

          E(u)=12(dudz)2+W(u)

          integral both side of the former equation

          ∫−∞+∞ddz(12(dudz)2+W)dz=∫−∞+∞−c(dudz)2dz(12(dudz)2+W)|−∞+∞=−c∫−∞+∞(dudz)2dz

          the boundary condition is limz→+∞u(z)=0,limz→−∞u(z)=1 , we also assume limz→+∞u′(z)=0,limz→−∞u′(z)=0

          W(u(+∞))−W(u(−∞))=−c∫−∞+∞(dudz)2dzW(1)−W(0)=−c∫−∞+∞(dudz)2dz
          c=W(0)−W(1)∫−∞+∞(dudz)2dz

          When a=12, W(0)=W(1), thus c=0. As a result,

          d2udz2=u(1−u)(0.5−u)
          u=11+ez2

          Also,

          ddz(12(dudz)2+W)=0

          And

          12(dudz)2+W=0dudz=2W(u)=22u(1−u)

           

          Now for other a≠1/2, we assume the shape of u(z) might be the same. thus dudz=22u(1−u). put back to c,

          c=W(0)−W(1)∫−∞+∞(dudz)2dz=W(0)−W(1)∫10(dudz)du=(a6−112)21∫10u(1−u)dz=2(12−a)

           

           

          Assume:

          u˙=ku(1−u)u¨=k(1−2u)⋅u˙=k2u(1−u)(1−2u)
          u¨+cu˙+u(1−u)(u−a)=0
          k2u(1−u)(1−2u)+cku(1−u)+u(1−u)(u−a)=0k2(u−3u2+2u3)+ck(u−u2)−au+(1+a)u2−u3=0(2k2−1)u3+(1+a−3k2−ck)u2+(k2+ck−a)u=0

          we have

          2k2+1=01+a−3k2−ck=0k2+ck−a=0

          thus k=22, nothing to do with a.

          Then

          a−12−22c=0⇒c=2(a−12)

          holds for any a.

          Two species

          ∂N1dt=D1∂2N1∂x2+N1(r1−A1,1N1−A1,2N2)∂N2dt=D2∂2N2∂x2+N2(r2−A2,2N2−A2,1N1)

           

          Symmetry

          We assume the condition where two species are exactly symmetric. The system could be simplified (as well as rescaled) as

          ∂N1=∇2N1+rN1(1−N1−aN2)∂N2=∇2N2+rN2(1−N2−aN1)

          let u=N1+N2, v=N1−N2

          ∂u=∇2u+ru−r(N12+N22)−2raN1N2∂v=∇2v+rv−r(N12−N22)

          Notice the fact that u2+v2=2(N12+N22), u2−v2=4N1N2, uv=N12−N22.

          ∂tu=∇x2u+ru−12r(u2+v2)−12ra(u2−v2)∂tv=∇x2v+rv−ruv=∇x2v+rv(1−u)

          If the system is neutral, then u=N1+N2 is a constant (here equals to 1), both u and v only change by dispersal.

          When a>1, the system without dispersal is a bistable system, two stable points are (u∗,v∗)=(1,0),(0,1).

          Now let us assume that space is still nearly fully occupied, then

          u=1−η

          and

          vu=ϕ(x)

           

          N1=u2(1+ϕ)N2=u2(1−ϕ)
          ∂tu=∇x2u+ru−1+a2ru2−1−a2rv2

          we might assume ∂tu=0 since the total population size is nearly a constant, and ∇x2u= 0

          since the space is fully occupied. then,

          1+a2u2−u+1−a2v2=0u2−21+au+1−a1+av2=0

          u=1±1+(a2−1)v21+a.

           

          ∂v=∇2v+rv(1−1±1+(a2−1)v21+a)

           

          This reaction term also has 3 root 0,1,−1, and also nearly the same shape of kv(1−v)(1+v).

          We write the Taylor expansion of f(v)/rv=(1−1+1+(a2−1)v21+a)

          f(v)/rv=(1−1+1+(a2−1)v21+a)=a1+a−11+a(1+(a2−1)v2)12=a1+a−11+a(1+(a2−1)v22+O(v4))≈a−1a+1−a−12v2
          ∂v=∇2v+r(a−1)a+1v(1−a+12v2)∂v=∇2v+(a−1)2(a+1)(a+12)ra+12v(1−a+12v2)

          also

          f′(v)=rv(1−a)v(1+(a2−1)v2)−12+r(1−1+1+(a2−1)v21+a)=rv(1−a)11v2+(a2−1)+r(1−1+1+(a2−1)v21+a)
          f′(0)=(a−1)r1+a

          for

          g(v)=rkv(1−v2)g′(v)=rk(1−3v2)g′(0)=rk

          Thus we could approximately set f(v)≈r′v(1−v2), where r′=ra−1a+1

          Thus, the shape of the interaction zone is

          u=11+er′2z,r′=ra−1a+1

           

           

           

          ！ 对每个物种分别考虑！关键是a
